etymology.description: 天(天) (tiān, "sky; heaven; god") is understood as the highest or divine authority in Chinese cosmology, representing the heavens or deities. 啊(啊) (a, "ah; oh") is a particle used to express surprise, exclamation, or emphasis. Combined, 天啊 (tiān a) expresses an exclamation of surprise or wonder, akin to saying "oh my god" in English.
etymology.interpretation: 天(天) (tiān, "sky; heaven; god") invokes the concept of the sky or heavens, often symbolizing divine power or presence. In traditional Chinese thought, the heavens are seen as overseeing or governing the world. 啊(啊) (a, "ah; oh") is an exclamatory particle used to convey emotions such as surprise. Together, 天啊 (tiān a) implies calling out to the heavens or expressing surprise with a sense of invoking higher powers, similar to an exclamation like "oh my god" in English.

examples.chinese: 天啊(天啊)，你看那座山有多高(你看那座山有多高)！
examples.pinyin: Tiān a, nǐ kàn nà zuò shān yǒu duō gāo!
examples.english: Oh my god, look at how tall that mountain is!

examples.chinese: 天啊(天啊)，我忘记带钥匙了(我忘記帶鑰匙了)！
examples.pinyin: Tiān a, wǒ wàngjì dài yàoshi le!
examples.english: Oh my god, I forgot to bring the keys!

examples.chinese: 天啊(天啊)，他竟然赢了比赛(他竟然贏了比賽)！
examples.pinyin: Tiān a, tā jìngrán yíng le bǐsài!
examples.english: Oh my god, he actually won the game!
